content: How many oxygen atoms are present in 0.36 g of a drop of water at STP?
explanation:
Let's break down the problem step-by-step:

Determine the Moles of Water

The molar mass of water (H₂O) is approximately:

$$M_{H_2O} = 2(1) + 16 = 18 \text{ g/mol}$$

For 0.36 g of water, the number of moles is:

$$n = \frac{\text{mass}}{\text{molar mass}} = \frac{0.36 \text{ g}}{18 \text{ g/mol}} = 0.02 \text{ mol}$$

Calculate the Number of Water Molecules

Using Avogadro’s number ($N_A \approx 6.02 \times 10^{23}$ molecules/mol), the total number of water molecules is:

$$\text{Number of molecules} = 0.02 \text{ mol} \times 6.02 \times 10^{23} \text{ molecules/mol} = 1.204 \times 10^{22} \text{ molecules}$$

Find the Number of Oxygen Atoms

Each water molecule has one oxygen atom. Therefore, the number of oxygen atoms equals the number of water molecules:

$$\text{Oxygen atoms} = 1.204 \times 10^{22}$$

Thus, the number of oxygen atoms present is:

$$1.205 \times 10^{22}$$

This matches Option B.

content:
Arrange the following in order of increasing mass

I. 1 mole of N$$_2$$

II. 0.5 mole of O$$_3$$

III. $$3.011\times10^{23}$$ molecules of O$$_2$$

IV. 0.5 gram atom of O$$_2$$
explanation:
(I) Mass of mole of $$\mathrm{N}_2$$ is given by

Mass $$=$$ Number of moles $$\times$$ molar mass

$$\begin{aligned} & =1 \times 28 \mathrm{~g} \\ & =28 \mathrm{~g} \end{aligned}$$

(II) Mass of 0.5 mole of $$\mathrm{O}_3$$

$$=0.5 \times 48=24 \mathrm{~g}$$

(III) Mass of $$3.011 \times 10^{23}$$ molecules of $$\mathrm{O}_2$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{2} \text { mole of } \mathrm{O}_2 \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \times 32=16 \mathrm{~g} \end{aligned}$$

(IV) Mass of $$0.5 \mathrm{~g}$$ atom of $$\mathrm{O}_2$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{2} \text { mole of atoms of } \mathrm{O} \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \times 16=8 \mathrm{~g} \end{aligned}$$

Thus, the order of increasing mass is

$$\text { IV }< \text { III }< \text { II }<\text { I }$$

content: 63 g of a compound (Mol. Wt. = 126) was dissolved in 500 g distilled water. The density of the resultant solution as 1.126 g/ml. The molarity of the solution is
explanation:
Given

Mass of compound (Solute) $$=63 \mathrm{~g}$$

Mass of solvent $$=\mathbf{5 0 0} \mathrm{g}$$

Molecular weight $$=126 \mathrm{~g}$$

Density $$=1.126 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mL}$$

Molarity $$=$$ ?

Mass of solution $$=$$ Mass of solute + Mass of solvent

$$=63+500=563 \mathrm{~g}$$

Number of moles $$=\frac{63}{126}=0.5$$ moles

Volume $$=\frac{\text { Mass }}{\text { Density }}=\frac{563}{1.126} \mathrm{~mL}$$

Now, using the formula of molarity,

$$\begin{aligned} M & =\frac{\text { Number of moles of solute }}{\text { Volume of solution (in L) }} \\ 1 & =\frac{0.5 \times 1000}{563 / 1.126} \\ 1 & =\frac{1.126 \times 1000 \times 0.5}{563}=1 \text { Molar or } 1.0 \mathrm{M} . \end{aligned}$$

content: How much solid oxalic acid (Molecular weight 126) has to be weighed to prepare 100 ml. exactly 0.1 (N) oxalic acid solution in water?
explanation:
Given, molecular weight of oxalic acid = 126

Volume = 100 mL

Normality = 0.1 N

Mass = ?

We know,

Normality $$=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{Mass}}{\mathrm{Equivalent\,weight}}}{\mathrm{Volume\, (Litre})}$$

($$\therefore$$ Equivalent weight $$=\frac{\mathrm{Molecular\,weight}}{\eta - \mathrm{factor}}=\frac{126}{2}$$ [$$\eta$$ -factor = 2 for oxalic acid)

$$\therefore 0.1=\frac{\mathrm{Mass}}{63\times\frac{100}{1000}}\Rightarrow \mathrm{Mass}=\frac{0.1\times63\times100}{1000}$$

$$\therefore$$ Mass = 0.63 g

content:
$${C_6}{H_6}(liq) + {{15} \over 2}{O_2}(g) \to 6C{O_2}(g) + 3{H_2}O(liq)$$

Benzene burns in oxygen according to the above equation. What is the volume of oxygen (at STP) needed for complete combustion of 39 gram of liquid benzene?
explanation:
$${C_6}{H_6}(l) + {{15} \over 2}{O_2}(g)\buildrel {} \over \longrightarrow 6C{O_2}(g) + 3{H_2}O(l)$$

78 g of C6H6 required = $${{15} \over 2}$$ mole of O2

39 g ($${1 \over 2}$$ mole) C6H6 requires $$ = {{15} \over 2} \times {1 \over 2}$$ mole of O2

$$ = {{15} \over 4}$$ mole of O2

$$\because$$ $$\mathrm{Moles}=\frac{\mathrm{Volume\,(L)}}{22.4}$$

$$\frac{15}{4}=\frac{\mathrm{Volume}}{22.4}$$

$$\therefore$$ Volume = 84 L (O2)

content: A metal (M) forms two oxides. The ratio M:O (by weight) in the two oxides are 25:4 and 25:6. The minimum value of atomic mass of M is
explanation:
Let the two oxides be M2Ox and M2Oy.

Let atomic weight of metal, M = a

$$\therefore$$ For MOx,

ratio of weight

2a : 16x = 25 : 4 ..... (i)

For MOy,

ratio of weight

2a : 16y = 25 : 6 ..... (ii)

From (i) and (ii), we get

$$\therefore$$ 16x : 16y = 4 : 6

$$\therefore$$ x : y = 2 : 3

$$\therefore$$ The two oxides may be M2O2 or M2O3.

Now, 2a : 16x2 = 25 : 4

$$\therefore$$ a = $$\frac{25}{4}\times16$$ = 100

content: 1 mL of water has 25 drops. Let N0 be the Avogadro number. What is the number of molecules present in 1 drop of water ? (Density of water = 1 g/mL)
explanation:
Number of drops in 1 mL water = 25

$$\therefore$$ Volume of 1 drop = $$\frac{1}{25}$$ mL

$$\therefore$$ Mass of water = Density $$\times$$ Volume

= 1 $$\times$$ $$\frac{1}{25}$$

= $$\frac{1}{25}$$ g

$$\therefore$$ Moles of water = $$\frac{\mathrm{Mass}}{\mathrm{Molecular\,mass}}$$

= $$\frac{1}{25\times18}$$

$$\therefore$$ Molecules of water = N0 $$\times$$ Moles of water

$$=\frac{N_0}{25\times18}=\frac{0.02}{9}N_0$$

content: The mole fraction of a solute in a binary solution is 0.1 at 298 K, molarity of this solution is same as its molality. Density of this solution at 298 K is 2.0 g cm<sup>$$-$$3</sup>. The ratio of molecular weights of the solute and the solvent (M<sub>solute</sub> / M<sub>solvent</sub>) is
explanation:
Given,

xsolute = 0.1

Molarity = Molality

Density of solution = 2g cm$$-$$3

= $${{2 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}} \over {{{10}^{ - 6}}}}$$ kg

= 2 $$\times$$ 103 kg/m3

= 2 kg/L

$${{{{{m_{solute}}} \over {{M_{solute}}}}} \over {Volume}} = {{{{{m_{solute}}} \over {{M_{solute}}}}} \over {Mass\,of\,solvent\,(kg)}}$$

Volume of solution = Mass of solvent (kg)

$${{Mass\,of\,solution} \over d}$$ = Mass of solvent

$$\therefore$$ $${{Mass\,of\,solution} \over {Mass\,of\,solvent}} = 2$$

$$\therefore$$ $${{Mass\,of\,solute + Mass\,of\,solvent} \over {Mass\,of\,solvent}} = 2$$

$$\therefore$$ $${{Mass\,of\,solute} \over {Mass\,of\,solvent}} = 1$$ .... (i)

$${{{\eta _{solute}}} \over {{\eta _{solute}} + {\eta _{solvent}}}} = 0.1$$

$${{{{{m_{solute}}} \over {{M_{solute}}}}} \over {{{{m_{solute}}} \over {{M_{solute}}}} + {{{m_{solvent}}} \over {{M_{solvent}}}}}} = 0.1$$

$${{{1 \over {{M_{solute}}}}} \over {{1 \over {{M_{solute}}}} + {1 \over {{M_{solvent}}}}}} = 0.1$$ {$$\because$$ Msolute = msolvent using (i)}

$$\therefore$$ $${{{m_{solute}}} \over {{M_{solvent}}}} = 9$$

content: The mole fraction of ethanol in water is 0.08. Its molality is
explanation:
Let, the number of moles of ethanol = n
The number of moles of water = N

Now, mole fraction of ethanol

$$({\chi _B}) = \left[ {{n \over {n + N}}} \right] = 0.08$$

Mole fraction of water ($$\chi $$A) = 1-mole fraction of ethanol = 0.92

Molality = $${{{\chi _B} \times 1000} \over {(1 - {\chi _B}) \times {M_A}}}$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$m = {{1000 \times mole\,fraction\,of\,ethanol} \over {(1 - mole\,fraction\,of\,ethanol)}} \times molecular\,mass\,of\,{H_2}O$$

= $${{1000 \times 0.08} \over {(1 - .08) \times 18}} = 4.83\,mol/kg$$

So, the correct option is (b)

content: 5 mL of 0.1 M Pb(NO<sub>3</sub>)<sub>2</sub> is mixed with 10 mL of 0.02 M KI. The amount of PbI<sub>2</sub> precipitated will be about
Which of the following has the largest number of atoms?
explanation:
Number of atoms = $${{mass} \over {molar\,mass}} \times {N_A} \times number\,of\,atoms\,in\,1\,mole.$$

The calculation of number of atoms in given options are as follow :

(a) Number of atoms in 1 g of Ag

= $${1 \over {107.87}} \times {N_A} \times 1 = 0.0092\,{N_A}$$

(b) Number of atoms in 1 g of Fe

= $${1 \over {55.85}} \times {N_A} \times 1 = 0.0179\,{N_A}$$

(c) Number of atoms in 1g of Cl2

= $${1 \over {70}} \times {N_A} \times 2 = 0.0285\,{N_A}$$

(d) Number of atoms in 1g of Mg

= $${1 \over {24.30}} \times {N_A} \times 1 = 0.0411\,{N_A}$$

Thus, 1g of Mg contains the maximum number of atoms among the given options.

content: What will be the mass of one atom of <sup>12</sup>C?
explanation:
We know,

mass of NA atoms = 12g of C or mass of 6.023 $$ \times $$ 1023 atoms = 12g

$$ \therefore $$ Mass of 1 atom = $${{12} \over {6.023 \times {{10}^{23}}}}g = 1.9923 \times {10^{ - 23}}g$$

So, the correct option is (b).

content: In the crystalline solid MSO<sub>4</sub> . nH<sub>2</sub>O of molar mass 250 g mol<sup>$$-$$1</sup>, the percentage of anhydrous salt is 64 by weight. The value of n is
explanation:
Mass of $${H_2}O = {{36} \over {100}} \times 250 = 90$$ g

Moles of $${H_2}O = {{90} \over {18}} = 5$$ mol

In the crystalline solid MSO4 . nH2O, the value of n is 5.

Hence, option (c) is correct answer.

content: At S.T.P. the volume of 7.5 g of a gas is 5.6 L. The gas is
explanation:
Given, mass of gas (W) = 7.5 g

Volume of gas at STP (V) = 5.6 L

$$\because$$ Moles of gas at STP = $${{V(L)} \over {22.4(L)}} = {W \over M}$$

Where M = molar mass of gas.

$$\therefore$$ $$M = {{W \times 22.4} \over V}$$

$$ = {{7.5 \times 22.4} \over {5.6}} = 30.00$$ mol$$-$$1

Among the given options

Molar mass (M) of

(a) NO = 14 + 16 = 30.00 g mol$$-$$1

(b) N2O = 28 + 16 = 44.00 g mol$$-$$1

(c) CO = 12 + 16 = 28.00 g mol$$-$$1

(d) CO2 = 12 + 32 = 44.00 g mol$$-$$1

$$\because$$ Molar mass of NO = 30 g mol$$-$$1

Thus, the given gas is NO.

Hence, (a) is the correct option.

content: How many moles of electrons will weigh one kilogram?
explanation:
Mass of an electron = 9.108 $$\times$$ 10$$-$$31 kg

Mass of one mole of electron = (9.108 $$\times$$ 10$$-$$31 $$\times$$ 6.023 $$\times$$ 1023) kg

Then, number of mole of electron in 1 kg

$$ = {1 \over {9.108 \times 6.023 \times {{10}^{ - 8}}}}$$

$$ = {1 \over {9.108 \times 6.023}} \times {10^8}$$ mole of e$$-$$

content: A metal M (specific heat 0.16) forms a metal chloride with 65% chlorine present in it. The formula of the metal chloride will be
explanation:
Given, specific heat = 0.16

Let metal chloride be MClx then,

$${{6.4} \over {specific\,heat}}$$ = Atomic weight of metal

$${{6.4} \over {0.16}}$$ = Atomic weight of metal

Atomic weight = 40

40 is the atomic weight of calcium. According to question, metal chloride (MClx) have $$ \approx $$ 65% chlorine present in it.

$${{x \times Atomic\,weight\,of\,chlorine} \over {40 + x \times Atomic\,weight\,of\,chlorine}} \times 100 = 65$$

$${{x \times 35.5} \over {40 + x \times 35.5}} \times 100 = 65$$

$$x = 2.09 \approx 2$$ (approx.)

So, the formula of metal chloride will be MCl2.

content: Which of the following has the dimension if [$$M{L^0}{T^{ - 2}}$$] ?
explanation:
$$\because$$ Surface tension (T) = Work done per unit area

$$\therefore$$ $$T = {{dW} \over {dA}}$$

$$ = {{F.\,dx} \over {dA}}$$ (FL$$-$$1 = MT$$-$$2)

$$\therefore$$ Dimension of surface tension = $$M{L^0}{T^{ - 2}}$$

content: In a flask, the weight ratio of CH<sub>4</sub>(g) and SO<sub>2</sub>(g) at 298 K and 1 bar is 1 : 2. The ratio of the number of molecules of SO<sub>2</sub>(g) and CH<sub>4</sub>(g) is
